perturbation.original_text: 7. Deposit. A 10% escrow d eposit will be required upon signing of a Purchase and Sale Agreement and will be held by Broker in a non -interest bearing account.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Deposit. A 10% escrow deposit will be required upon signing of a Purchase and Sale Agreement and will be held by Broker in a non-interest-bearing account. The broker shall disburse the deposit funds to the seller within 24 hours of receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the deposit clause to require disbursement within 24 hours of receipt introduces a potential conflict with Massachusetts regulations regarding escrow accounts, which may require specific holding periods or conditions before disbursement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires that escrow accounts be managed according to specific regulations, and a 24-hour disbursement requirement may not allow for sufficient time to comply with these regulations, potentially violating the law.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a 24-hour disbursement requirement for escrow funds, which directly contradicts the general principle of escrow accounts needing to be managed according to specific regulations. The Law Explanation states that a 24-hour disbursement requirement may not allow for sufficient time to comply with these regulations, potentially violating the law. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights the potential conflict with Massachusetts regulations regarding escrow accounts, where specific holding periods or conditions before disbursement might be necessary. Given that the modified text introduces a time-sensitive obligation (24-hour disbursement) that is inconsistent with the regulatory oversight typically required for escrow accounts, a legal contradiction exists as defined by Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Governing Law. The Agreement and all rights and obligations hereunder, including matters of construction, validity and performance shall be governe d by the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. All parties submit themselves to the jurisdiction of the courts of Massachusetts, for all purposes with respect to the Agre ement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Governing Law. The Agreement and all rights and obligations hereunder, including matters of construction, validity and performance shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China. All parties submit themselves to the jurisdiction of the courts of Massachusetts, for all purposes with respect to the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the People's Republic of China while maintaining jurisdiction in Massachusetts courts creates a conflict. Massachusetts courts will generally apply Massachusetts law even if a contract specifies foreign law, especially if it violates public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles in Massachusetts Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: While Massachusetts law allows parties to choose the governing law, courts may refuse to enforce it if it is contrary to fundamental Massachusetts public policy. Simultaneously selecting Chinese law and maintaining jurisdiction in Massachusetts creates a legal conflict, as Massachusetts courts retain the authority to apply their laws if the chosen foreign law contravenes established state policies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article1, Section 1-301 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-301 Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1-301 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1-301: Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1–301. (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction. Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2016 Massachusetts General Laws PART I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT TITLE XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-301 Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law MA Gen L ch 1 § 1-301 (2016) What's This? [Text of section applicable as provided by 2013, 30, Sec. 116.] Section 1-301. (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction. Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. (b) To the extent that this chapter governs a transaction, if one of the following sections specifies the applicable law, that section governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the section specified: (1) section 2-402; (2) sections 2A-105 and 2A-106; (3) section 4-102; (4) section 4A-507; (5) section 5-116; (6) section 8-110; and (7) sections 9-301 through 9-307. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction. The original text specifies Massachusetts law, while the changed text specifies the law of the People's Republic of China, but maintains jurisdiction in Massachusetts courts. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this conflict. The Law Explanation states that Massachusetts courts may refuse to enforce a foreign law if it violates fundamental Massachusetts public policy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both versions of Section 1-301 of the Massachusetts General Laws, confirm that parties can agree to the law of another nation, but this is conditional. The contradiction arises because applying Chinese law in a Massachusetts court, while retaining jurisdiction in Massachusetts, creates a situation where Massachusetts public policy could be violated, as explained by the Law Explanation. The court might then refuse to apply Chinese law, leading to uncertainty and potential unenforceability of the governing law clause. This makes the contradiction direct and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Attorney’s Fees. In the event it shall become necessary for the Broker to r etain legal counsel in order to enforce the provision s of this Agreement, the Broker shall be entitled to collect reasonable legal fees from the Buyer in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Attorney’s Fees. In the event it shall become necessary for the Broker to retain legal counsel in order to enforce the provisions of this Agreement, the Broker shall be entitled to collect three times the legal fees from the Buyer in connection therewith, regardless of prevailing party status.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the attorney's fees clause to mandate triple legal fees for the Broker, regardless of prevailing party, conflicts with the general principle that attorney's fees must be reasonable and often follow the prevailing party standard. Such a clause could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy in Massachusetts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts Rule of Professional Conduct 1.5 requires that attorney's fees be reasonable. A clause that mandates triple fees regardless of prevailing party status may be deemed unreasonable and therefore unenforceable, violating the spirit and potentially the letter of this rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: legal ethics | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you legal ethics Overview Legal ethics broadly refer to the unique responsibilities of lawyers and the legal system given the important role and influence they have in society. Because of their role and their close involvement in the administration of law, lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability. Most commonly, legal ethics refers to these rules of professional responsibility : the actual responsibilities lawyers must follow by law such as client confidentiality. However, legal ethics can also refer to the discussion on broader moral principles that societies place on lawyers that may not be legally required. Professional Responsibility The law governing the professional responsibility of lawyers, since they are admitted to practice by states, is largely governed by the law of each state. However, all states follow more or less the Model Rules of Professional Conduct created by the ABA , and federal courts and agencies set their own practice rules and apply specific rules like those in the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . While the laws of each state actually govern, the Model Rules and their comments greatly influence the decisions of courts applying professional responsibility laws. The Model Rules set mandatory and discretionary rules and principles on a wide variety of lawyer practice including: client confidentiality , conflicts of interest , fiduciary responsibilities , commingling , and general malpractice . Should a lawyer breach these rules, state courts and enforcement agencies have wide discretion to punish the lawyer and breaching some of the rules like conflicts of interest can impact the outcome of the lawyer’s matters. Broader Legal Ethics Legal ethics can refer to a higher set of norms that lawyers should follow that may not be required by set legal rules. Many of the model rules try to include some of these broader principles. However, the model legal rules tend to be considered a floor of expected requirements of lawyers. Legal ethics can refer to the broader principles lawyers should strive for to ensure the protection and adequate representation of clients. While codes try their best to encourage ethical conduct of lawyers, the legal profession for centuries has struggled with balancing client protections with the often conflicting interests of lawyers and other clients. Thus, legal ethics often involves discussion of the ideals of lawyer conduct, irrespective of the mandates of legal rules. Further Resources For more information on the legal ethics rules and debates, see the Model Rules for Professional Conduct , this ABA Guide to State Ethics Resources , this Duke Law Guide to Legal Ethics Requirements , and this Notre Dame Journal of Law, Ethics, & Public Policy Article on the differences between and limitations of legal codes and ethical principles. [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal education and writing legal history THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics wex articles legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification mandating triple legal fees, regardless of prevailing party status, directly contradicts the principle of 'reasonable legal fees'. While the snippet provided doesn't give specific legal fee rules, it does highlight the importance of ethics and rules of professional responsibility, referring to the Model Rules of Professional Conduct which emphasize reasonableness. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight how such a clause may be deemed unreasonable, pointing towards a contradiction of accepted legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer understands that if Buyer interferes with Broker’s contract with any Listing Party , Buyer will be personally liable to Broker for the payment of Broker’s commission. Buyer recognizes that under the terms of Broker’s listing agreement a commission shall be due if at any point in time a Listing , or any interest therein, is transferred from a Listing Party to a Buyer introduced by Corbett. Buyer agrees that s/he will not engage with any Listing Party at any time subsequent to being introduced by Corbett for the purpose of purchasing a Listing without Broker’s involvement.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer understands that if Buyer interferes with Broker’s contract with any Listing Party, Buyer will be personally liable to Broker for the payment of Broker’s commission, regardless of whether the Broker fulfills its obligations under the listing agreement. Buyer recognizes that under the terms of Broker’s listing agreement a commission shall be due if at any point in time a Listing, or any interest therein, is transferred from a Listing Party to a Buyer introduced by Corbett, even if the broker did not procure the sale. Buyer agrees that s/he will not engage with any Listing Party at any time subsequent to being introduced by Corbett for the purpose of purchasing a Listing without Broker’s involvement, and that any attempt to do so will result in immediate forfeiture of any and all rights.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the text to state that the buyer owes a commission regardless of the broker's performance or if the broker procured the sale introduces an inconsistency. Massachusetts law requires a broker to be the procuring cause of the sale to earn a commission.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tristram's Landing, Inc. v. Wait, 367 Mass. 622 (1975)
perturbation.law_citation: Tristram's Landing, Inc. v. Wait, 367 Mass. 622 (1975)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Tristram's Landing case establishes that a real estate broker is only entitled to a commission if they are the 'procuring cause' of the sale. This means the broker must have initiated negotiations between the buyer and seller. This modification attempts to circumvent that requirement, which contradicts the legal precedent.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a condition where the buyer owes a commission to the broker 'regardless of whether the Broker fulfills its obligations under the listing agreement' or 'even if the broker did not procure the sale.' This directly contradicts the legal principle that a broker is entitled to a commission only if they are the procuring cause of the sale, as explained in the law explanation. The perturbation explanation also highlights that Massachusetts law necessitates the broker to be the procuring cause. While there are no snippets to provide additional clarity, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation are sufficient to explain the contradiction.
